content:
This is a pain point I've personally had many times when writing Elm, but I have no idea if it's just me. So now I'm just putting it out there to see how it goes :big_smile:

The key pain is:
When writing a type consisting of many tags, where the tags have some kind of ordering or grouping in their declaration, I want code that pattern matches on them to have the branches in the same order as the type declares the tags. This makes it much easier to navigate in the pattern match in my experience. It mainly happens with large Msg types, where tags are often grouped informally (i.e. some messages relate to initialization, some relate to a specific piece of UI, and so on). It's also not usually a problem in the early states of writing the code, because it's easy to keep them in sync there. My experience is that they tend to drift as the code gets updated by different people over long periods of time, and I often want to read the pattern match by these groupings. And the grouping gets maintained well in the original type declaration, but various pattern matches do not of course.

This of course only goes when matching on the type doesn't have an inherent ordering you need to use (if you have a default case you can't do full reordering for example).

And naturally there are many kinds of types where I don't care about the ordering, so it's not a universal thing for all types.

So I guess the questions are:

  • Does anyone else feel the same about this?
  • Would this be a decent fit for automatic formatting?
  • Is there some heuristic for deciding when a type should dictate pattern match ordering of tags? Maybe it's just when a type gets enough tags in it that some grouping naturally starts to occur (e.g. more than seven tags enforces ordering)

Kasper Møller Andersen said:

When writing a type consisting of many tags, where the tags have some kind of ordering or grouping in their declaration, I want code that pattern matches on them to have the branches in the same order as the type declares the tags.

unfortunately, changing the order can have performance implications, so this isn't really something the formatter should do automatically - otherwise certain performance improvements would become impossible :sweat_smile:

What cases would that be? I can see how it matters for various list destructurings or if you’re using particular guards. But that could be part of a heuristic for when to order.

For example, if you pattern match on a type where you match on the same tag multiple times (with various sub conditions in each), we might not reorder those branches individually, but move them around together, so they keep the same order between them.
for small numbers of branches they basically compile to the same thing as if/else conditionals
so the number of total comparisons that are made at runtime can depend on the order in which they appear

Same if the branches are too complex (like matching subfields, having guard clauses, or mapping tons of values to _ -> ... making the check sparse). For an enum style use should always be a jump table (though even that is up to llvm).

I would actually do the reverse of this. I would go back and declare the Tags in the order in which they will appear in whens. Don't optimize early, but think "Which of these tags will probably occur more often?", and then order them in the declaration appropriately.

1) I wanna disregard performance for a second and hear what your thoughts are on this idea if it didn't have any implications for runtime performance. Of course we will need to specify some rules around what happens when you're matching on subfields, guards, etc., but I just want to assume that we can figure out a good set of rules for those cases (including when to bail out of reordering completely, in case it's not properly decidable). What would your thoughts be around having this as part of Roc in such a scenario?

2) Putting performance back in the picture, I'm a bit worried that we're saying that (a) we expect people to want to order match branches for performance reasons, and (b) the performance characteristic of pattern matching is basically up to LLVM heuristics, which are a black box that will potentially change with every LLVM update. Have we considered giving people more explicit control over what the compiler does for the cases where it actually matters, and then just leaving normal pattern matching to LLVM to do what it considers best?

Looking at Rust again, you've got solutions like #[hot] and #[cold], and if-let chains to let people exercise better control over what happens, while still having destructuring like in a pattern match.

A more Roc-like solution might something like a when myThing is ordered instead of just when myThing is. So it still looks like a normal pattern match, but the ordered keyword (or however it would look) basically has a forced performance characteristic, where the pattern match must behave like a bunch of if/else statements. So now people can better exercise control when they need it, and if we were to have the formatter reorder tags, it wouldn't touch ordered pattern matches of course. Though I think this makes sense even without the reordering formatter.

Anthony Bullard sagde:

I would actually do the reverse of this. I would go back and declare the Tags in the order in which they will appear in whens. Don't optimize early, but think "Which of these tags will probably occur more often?", and then order them in the declaration appropriately.

That would only work if the performance characteristics are the same at all use sites though, which I don't think we can expect :thinking:
That's TOTALLY true

Kasper Møller Andersen said:

1) I wanna disregard performance for a second and hear what your thoughts are on this idea if it didn't have any implications for runtime performance.

my thoughts are that formatting shouldn't change performance, so I don't really have any thoughts on an idea for formatting that could change performance :sweat_smile:
it's kinda like "setting aside the goal of having the formatter be zero-config, if it did have config options, what would they be?"
I don't have any thoughts on that either, because it just is a goal to be zero-config, just like it's a goal to not change performance :big_smile:
I mean, golly, if only my point number two had been a suggestion that might make both performance and formatting nicer :wink:

Kasper Møller Andersen said:

2) Putting performance back in the picture, I'm a bit worried that we're saying that (a) we expect people to want to order match branches for performance reasons, and (b) the performance characteristic of pattern matching is basically up to LLVM heuristics, which are a black box that will potentially change with every LLVM update. Have we considered giving people more explicit control over what the compiler does for the cases where it actually matters, and then just leaving normal pattern matching to LLVM to do what it considers best?

Looking at Rust again, you've got solutions like #[hot] and #[cold], and if-let chains to let people exercise better control over what happens, while still having destructuring like in a pattern match.

I don't think Rust allows any more control over this than we do, other than hot/cold annotations as hints that the optimizer only uses to inform its heuristics.

I think the main thing llvm does is to either generate a jump table or to generate multiple conditional jumps, and it has heuristics based on the branching for when to do that or not. Rust doesn't let you control that and I don't think Roc should either.

as I understand it, if you put the least likely branch at the end of a when, that will always be either neutral or positive for performance (neutral if jump table, positive if multiple jumps) - that's already true, doesn't require new language features, and is pretty intuitive I think

Kasper Møller Andersen said:

2) Putting performance back in the picture, I'm a bit worried that we're saying that (a) we expect people to want to order match branches for performance reasons, and (b) the performance characteristic of pattern matching is basically up to LLVM heuristics, which are a black box that will potentially change with every LLVM update.

a) I don't think so. I would expect it to be pretty rare, but it can matter. Most people don't count if branches or avoid short circuit conditionals for performance. I think it is the same for when ... is. Almost no one will care. But it is regularly seen in the wild that when it does matter it can have huge perf implications. In the worst case a switch is a linear list of if conditions. So branch m only ever gets executed after testing branch 0 to n. So having the most run branch first can be very important in that case.

b) yeah, lots of perf is up to the whims of llvm. This is why profiling is so important. There are nobs to play with, but I don't think this is a problem for us to try and solve currently. Rust and c++ both have these problems (bar inline assembly that essentially no one recommends using for perf), but manage great perf anyway. Mojo is looking at trying to solve some of these problems, but it is all way too fine grain and detail heavy to fit a language like roc.

My suggestion would still technically allow formatting to change performance, but only in the case where you specify that you don’t care. And having written Scala in two different jobs over many years, I have never, ever, written a pattern match where I cared about the performance of the match as a whole. It’s always been about logic and readability. I’m not saying there aren’t cases where on cares about pattern match performance, but simply that having the general case be focused on readability (which includes ordering in my view) is much higher value for the average developer :blush:

Yeah, this feels like a case where I would vote for a one off formatter flag to reorder switches if someone really wants to. But them you have to also protect the switches where perf matters and I don't like that.
And yeah, for most people perf almost never matters at the level of language features.
More about high level algorithm and design

The reason I put if-let in the same bucket, is that it essentially allows you write a pattern match with clearer semantics on how the branches should behave, if you don’t like what the normal pattern matching is producing. LLVM can still do with it as it pleasers of course, but it gives you a way to express your constraints differently
Sounds like in the far future, this would be a better use case for something like PGO to hint at where the reorderings should occur

Kasper Møller Andersen said:

The reason I put if-let in the same bucket, is that it essentially allows you write a pattern match with clearer semantics on how the branches should behave, if you don’t like what the normal pattern matching is producing. LLVM can still do with it as it pleasers of course, but it gives you a way to express your constraints differently

I always thought of if-let as being for performing an effect in a single case

Anthony Bullard sagde:

I always thought of if-let as being for performing an effect in a single case

Sure, but it works for both cases:big_smile:

Richard Feldman sagde:

as I understand it, if you put the least likely branch at the end of a when, that will always be either neutral or positive for performance (neutral if jump table, positive if multiple jumps) - that's already true, doesn't require new language features, and is pretty intuitive I think

And I appreciate the simplicity of it, but it really prioritizes that I should write my pattern match with concern for performance first instead of readability in all cases where I don’t care about performance (which is the common case). I can of course write my matches however I want, but the lack of this kind of guaranteed reordering is one of my big pain points with readability today. So I would personally rather bolster the readability side, and give the performance side a way to opt out when needed. However that should look :blush:

For reference, my Elm experience comes from a single web application of several hundred thousand lines of code, with some 20-30 developers making regular changes to it. In many smaller applications, I suspect the issue will be much smaller :smile:
I guess it's a personal preference thing, but it never actually occurred to me to consider a when less readable if its branches are in a different order from its definition :big_smile:
it's just not something that I have any positive or negative feelings about
obviously others can feel differently though!

It’s mainly about the navigational aspect of it, i.e. knowing that when I’m looking at branch X in my pattern match, I can easily find branch Y, because they are grouped together in the type definition, because they relate to the same thing. It just means less scrolling and searching to find what I’m looking for, which is very helpful when you have 40+ tags jumbled together :sweat_smile:

I use a single rule for ordering of when branches, "the largest block should come last" that's basically all i every care about.
purely because it is easy to loose track of a tiny trailing error handler

And it would be important for any kind of ordering to not touch a case like that. Which is why I think there should, at the very least, be some kind of heuristic for selecting when to apply the ordering. And if no satisfactory heuristic can be found, some kind of opt-in to ordering would be best. I know this also goes counter to the zero-config formatting, though maybe it should be considered separately from that. Whether as roc organise, or with linting, or something else.
